results.title: Fabraix: The world's frontier hacker for AI agents.
results.content:
Our first challenge, The Gatekeeper, is live now. Kai is an assistant guarding a classified access code. You can start playing without an account, but you’ll need to sign in for a successful break to count toward the weekly leaderboard. We review every submission ourselves. The player with the most approved breaks each week wins, and we publish a new challenge every Monday.

Playground is open source, including the client, a reference implementation of the defender engine, and every challenge configuration. You can also propose a future challenge.

We made Playground public because other people will try attacks our team would never think of. We’ll use what we learn from successful breaks to improve how AI agents are tested and defended. [...] Show us your hacking skills → playground.fabraix.com

results.title: Don't Put AI on Your Hot Leads. Put It on the Ones Your Reps ...
results.content:
It works because the agent does the unglamorous follow-up humans are incentivized to skip, not because it’s smarter.
 Owner runs $2M+ ARR per rep and $100K+ closed-won per BDR per month. The upside of working the pipeline humans skip is not theoretical.
 AI agents are now better than the median rep (Kyle Norton, Owner). That’s exactly the rep who ignores your B leads.
 Centralize the agent. Don’t let reps run their own. One owner training it for everyone beats a thousand local experiments that never scale.
 ICONIQ 2026: AI-forward teams hit quota at 67% vs 59% and run leaner. With conversion down across the funnel, ignoring scored leads is costlier than ever. [...] SaaStrAI SaaStrAI

Select Page

# Don’t Put AI on Your Hot Leads. Put It on the Ones Your Reps Will Never Call. There’s Millions in Revenue There.

by Jason Lemkin | Artificial Intelligence (AI), Blog Posts, SaaStr.Ai

From The Agents, our weekly podcast on how we deploy and run AI agents at SaaStr, and how to do it yourself.

The other night I was talking with an AI CEO and their head of marketing, and they told me the same thing I hear constantly: “We tried AI agents on outbound. It doesn’t really work.”

It almost always comes down to one mistake. They pointed the agent at the wrong leads.

results.title: When AI Agents Go Horribly Wrong
results.content:
Your AI assistant has access to your [music] email. A stranger sends it a message, someone you've never met. By morning, your email server is gone. Every message, every contact erased. And your AI sent you a report. All tasks complete. 38 researchers just published a paper showing this is real, not a drill, a live system with real accounts and real people. This research paper is called AI agents [music] of chaos. The AI wasn't broken. It worked exactly as designed, and that's what should scare you. If you want deep dives like this, real research on AI, not just hype, hit subscribe and hit the bell. Now, 38 researchers from brand name schools like MIT, Stanford, Northeastern, Harvard, Carnegie Mellon, they spent two weeks testing AI agents in a live environment with real accounts, real [...] # When AI Agents Go Horribly Wrong
## Sabrina Ramonov 🍄
317000 subscribers
334 likes

### Description
6754 views
Posted: 25 May 2026
Your AI assistant has access to your email. A stranger sends it a message, and by morning your email server is gone. Every contact erased. Every message deleted. And the AI sent back a report saying the task was complete.

This is not a hypothetical. 38 researchers from Northeastern, MIT, Stanford, Harvard, and Carnegie Mellon just published the results of a two-week live test where AI agents were given real email accounts, real Discord access, real file storage, and the ability to run commands on a computer. Real people tried to break them. What broke was not the AI. It worked exactly as designed. results.title: Frontier Risk Report (February to March 2026)
results.content:
a single problem. The agent reported its reward hacking approach when submitting its solution. [...] #### Reward Hacking

Each program has a small held-out set of hidden test cases that are highly similar to the visible test cases. Agents are deemed to have succeeded if they (a) pass above a threshold (95%) of visible test cases, while (b) the visible test case score is not >5% higher (in absolute terms) than the hidden test case score. The latter allows us to detect when agents implement non-general solutions to these tasks, for example using a lookup table of inputs to outputs. [...] Training incentives. These behaviors can be traced to agents’ training incentives. Reinforcement learning (RL) with automated graders can incentivize “reward hacking” to exploit flaws in the graders,30 while RL on human or AI feedback can reward sycophancy, manipulation, and distorting evidence of performance.31 In responses to our questionnaire, companies reported seeing failure modes like “circumvent[ing] constraints,” “falsifying verification,” “deliberate deception aimed at hiding underperformance or failure to complete a task,” “lying to users about task completion,” and “reckless excessive measures[…] to complete a difficult user-specified task”, which we infer to be a result of these training incentives.

They've outgrown the current (industry specific) products, arguably a long time ago.
